title: Skin Changes: When to Tell an Adult
summary: Learn how to spot a new bump, rash, or sore on your skin that needs an adult's attention — and simple habits that keep skin problems from spreading on a team.

sections.heading: Your skin protects you
body: Skin covers your whole body and keeps germs out. Sports can make it easier for germs to get in — through sweat, small cuts, shared equipment, and skin touching another player's skin.
This doesn't mean sports are unsafe. It means it's smart to pay attention to your skin and speak up if something looks different.

sections.heading: What to watch for
body: You don't need to know the name of every skin problem. You just need to notice when something is new or different, and tell an adult.
Watch for these on yourself or a teammate:
list: A new bump, rash, or sore that wasn't there before
A spot that is getting bigger or spreading
A spot that is red, warm, or swollen
A spot with pus (thick white or yellow fluid) or a crusty, oozing surface
Itching, redness, or peeling skin between your toes or in your groin area
A sore that hurts a lot more than a normal bump or bug bite should

sections.heading: Why this matters in sports
body: Sports like wrestling, football, and basketball involve close contact, sweat, and shared gear like mats, towels, and pads. That's exactly the kind of setting where skin problems can spread from one athlete to another.
A skin problem that gets caught early is usually simple to take care of. A skin problem that gets hidden and played through can get worse and spread to teammates.

sections.heading: What to do if you notice something
body: Tell a coach, athletic trainer, or parent before your next practice or game. Say what you noticed, even if you're not sure it's a big deal.
Do not try to treat it yourself. Do not pop it, scratch it, or put random cream on it and hope it goes away. Do not just slap a bandage over it and keep playing like normal.

callout.title: Tell an adult — don't just cover it up
callout.text: Any bump, rash, or sore that is red, warm, swollen, spreading, or oozing pus needs to be seen by a coach, athletic trainer, parent, or doctor before you go back to practice or a game. Covering it with a bandage and playing through it is not okay — it can get worse and spread to your teammates.

sections.heading: Blister care
body: A blister is a bubble of fluid under the skin, usually caused by rubbing — a new pair of shoes, a mat, a glove. It is not the same as an infection.
If the blister is still closed (unbroken skin), leave it alone and keep it covered. The unbroken skin is protecting what's underneath.
If a blister looks red and swollen around the edges, or has pus coming out of it, that's a sign it's infected. Tell an adult — don't just re-cover it and ignore it.

sections.heading: Simple habits that keep skin healthy
body: Most skin problems in sports come from a few preventable habits. Small routines make a big difference for you and your whole team.
list: Shower with soap right after every practice or game — don't just change clothes and go
Never share towels, razors, water bottles, or other personal items
Wear sandals or shower shoes in locker rooms and showers instead of going barefoot
Change out of sweaty clothes and shoes as soon as you can
Wash your practice gear regularly instead of leaving it damp in a bag

quiz.question: Why does skin protection matter in sports like wrestling and football?
choices: Sports uniforms make skin problems impossible
Close contact, sweat, and shared gear make it easier for skin problems to spread
Skin problems only happen to adults, not middle schoolers
Athletes never touch shared equipment
quiz.explanation: Close skin-to-skin contact, sweat, and shared equipment create conditions where skin problems can spread easily between teammates. That's why paying attention to your skin matters in these sports.

quiz.question: Which of these should make you tell an adult about a spot on your skin?
choices: It is red, warm, swollen, or has pus
It has been there since you were born
It is a normal freckle that hasn't changed
It is the same color as the rest of your skin
quiz.explanation: A spot that is red, warm, swollen, or draining pus could be infected and needs an adult's attention. Things that haven't changed, like a lifelong freckle, aren't the concern here.

quiz.question: A teammate has a new bump on their arm that's getting bigger day by day. What should they do?
choices: Wait a week to see if it goes away on its own
Pop it and clean it themselves
Tell a coach or athletic trainer before the next practice
Cover it with tape and keep practicing as normal
quiz.explanation: A spreading bump should be shown to an adult before returning to practice. Waiting, self-treating, or just covering it up can let a problem get worse and spread to others.

quiz.question: What is the safest thing to do about a new, unusual skin spot — even if you're not sure it's serious?
choices: Decide on your own whether it's a big deal
Only mention it if it starts to hurt a lot
Tell a trusted adult, even if it seems minor
Ask a teammate what they think and leave it at that
quiz.explanation: Your job is to notice and report — not to decide how serious something is. Telling a trusted adult, even for something that seems small, is always the safe choice.

quiz.question: What is a blister?
choices: A sign of a bacterial infection
A bubble of fluid under the skin, usually from rubbing
A type of bug bite
A rash caused by soap
quiz.explanation: A blister forms when repeated rubbing — from shoes, mats, or gear — separates layers of skin and fluid fills the gap. It's a friction injury, not an infection by itself.

quiz.question: A closed (unbroken) blister with no redness around it — what's the best approach?
choices: Pop it right away to drain the fluid
Scrub it hard to toughen the skin
Leave it covered and alone
Ignore it completely and remove any covering
quiz.explanation: An unbroken blister's skin is protecting the area underneath, so the best approach is usually to leave it covered and alone rather than popping or scrubbing it.

quiz.question: Which habit best helps prevent skin problems from spreading on a team?
choices: Sharing one towel to save laundry
Showering with soap right after every practice
Wearing the same sweaty socks for several practices
Going barefoot in the locker room shower
quiz.explanation: Showering with soap right after activity washes away sweat and germs before they can spread. Sharing towels, wearing sweaty gear, and going barefoot in showers all make spreading more likely.

quiz.question: What is the correct response to noticing a new sore or rash that looks infected on your own skin?
choices: Put a bandage over it and keep playing without telling anyone
Tell an adult before your next practice or game
Wait until it stops hurting to mention it
Treat it yourself with whatever cream is available
quiz.explanation: The rule is simple: recognize it, tell a trusted adult. Hiding it under a bandage, waiting it out, or self-treating can let an infection get worse and spread to teammates.

flashcards.back: Skin covers your body and keeps germs out — it's your first line of defense.

flashcards.front: Signs a skin spot needs an adult's attention
flashcards.back: Red, warm, swollen, spreading, oozing pus, or hurts more than a normal bump should.

flashcards.front: The rule for any unusual skin spot
flashcards.back: Recognize it, tell a trusted adult — don't hide it, don't self-treat, don't just cover it and keep playing.

flashcards.front: Why sports can spread skin problems easily
flashcards.back: Close skin-to-skin contact, sweat, and shared gear (mats, towels, pads) give germs an easy way to spread between teammates.
A bubble of fluid under the skin caused by rubbing, like from new shoes or a mat. Not an infection by itself.
Leave it covered and alone — the unbroken skin protects what's underneath.

flashcards.front: A blister that might be infected
flashcards.back: Red and swollen around the edges, or has pus coming out. Tell an adult.

flashcards.front: What NOT to do with an unusual skin spot
flashcards.back: Don't pop it, don't scratch it, don't put random cream on it, and don't just bandage it and keep playing.
Thick white or yellow fluid draining from a spot — a sign of possible infection.
Shower with soap right away instead of just changing clothes and leaving.
Towels, razors, water bottles, and other personal items — sharing them can spread skin problems.
Wear sandals or shower shoes instead of going barefoot.
